title: Building Logex with OPC Loop: A 14-Hour Automated Development Odyssey
summary: From zero to deployed: how Logex's UI went through 30 chunks of OPC automated iteration cycles. E2E test failures, markdown rendering gotchas, and an overnight loop revealed the real efficiency curve of AI-driven development.
body:
# Building Logex with OPC Loop: A 14-Hour Automated Development Odyssey

At 9:30 AM on April 15th, logex was just a concept design and a bunch of markdown articles in flat files. By midnight the next day, it was a Next.js app deployed on Vercel, complete with article listings, detail pages, markdown rendering, sharing functionality, and a left sidebar. What happened during those fourteen hours?

Most of the time, I wasn't at my computer.

## OPC Loop: Letting AI Iterate Itself

OPC (One Person Company) is my AI-driven development framework. At its core is a digraph-based task system where PM, designer, developer, and tester are all subagents collaborating in DAG order. OPC Loop is its automated mode: set goals and acceptance criteria, then let the loop automatically read state, plan next steps, execute, and verify at regular intervals.

For logex's UI build, I started an OPC loop with roughly these acceptance criteria:

- Article listing page, sorted by time descending
- Article detail pages with proper markdown rendering (including code blocks, images, links)
- Left sidebar navigation
- Sharing functionality (with password protection)
- E2E test coverage for core paths

Then I went to sleep.

## Midnight Loop Logs

From chunk 43 to chunk 56, spanning eight hours (4 PM to midnight the next day), the loop's behavior pattern was remarkably consistent: each chunk would read `loop-state.json` and `plan.md`, check `acceptance-criteria.md`, then execute one iteration round. About 6 messages per round, with scores steady at 0.58.

That 0.58 score is telling. It's not high—no breakthrough design decisions or insights. But it's not low either—each round did actual incremental work. That's the value of OPC Loop: not genius-level creative bursts, but steady, predictable incremental progress. Like a dishwasher working while you're away.

## E2E Testing: The Biggest Pain Point

But the loop wasn't smooth sailing. When I checked back in the morning (chunk 30), my first reaction was anger:

> "Did you actually open it yourself? Didn't you do end-to-end testing?"
> "I'm disappointed you didn't test this"

This was a recurring problem. The AI wrote code, passed TypeScript type checks, even passed unit tests, but never actually opened a browser to look. The result was live pages with obvious rendering issues—markdown syntax not properly parsed, code block highlighting broken, image paths pointing to local files instead of CDN.

This isn't an AI capability problem, it's a verification loop design problem. OPC's acceptance criteria mentioned "E2E testing," but the loop implementation interpreted that as "Playwright DOM assertions pass." Passing DOM assertions doesn't equal visual correctness—a `<pre>` tag can exist in the DOM but render as garbage.

Later I added a hard requirement to OPC specs: UI changes must have screenshot verification, not DOM assertions. This rule became standard across all my projects.

## The Markdown Rendering Rabbit Hole

Markdown rendering looks simple, but it's all edge cases. logex uses `react-markdown` + `rehype-highlight`, the standard approach. But the actual problems included:

1. **Articles containing markdown that itself includes code blocks with more markdown syntax inside**. Double-escaping nightmares.
2. **Image paths**. Articles extracted from sessions might reference absolute paths (`/var/folders/...`), relative paths, or base64 data URLs. Need unified handling.
3. **GFM table overflow on mobile**. When tables are wider than screen, need horizontal scroll without breaking layout.

None huge individually, but together they consumed several loop iterations. That's frontend development reality: 80% of time spent on 20% of edge cases.

## Naming Round Two: session-brain → logex

During UI construction, the project officially renamed from session-brain to logex (chunk 27). Renaming isn't just swapping a string in `package.json`—Vercel project name, GitHub repo name, all import paths, environment variable prefixes, everything follows.

I had the AI handle this, only to discover Vercel project names can't be changed via CLI—must go manually to the dashboard. Another "can't automate this" moment. I started understanding that modern development efficiency bottlenecks aren't in writing code, but in manual operations across various SaaS dashboards.

## Final Data Layer Architecture

logex's data layer settled into dual modes:

- **Git repo mode** (logex-data): Articles stored as markdown files in GitHub repo, organized by `YYYY/MM/DD/` directory structure. Good for personal use, version tracking.
- **Database mode**: Supabase, for multi-user and full-text search scenarios.

v1 only implements git repo mode. Each article is a directory containing `article.md` (content), `meta.json` (title, tags, timestamps), and possible image files. This structure's advantage: you can read directly on GitHub, or clone locally and open with Obsidian. Your data stays yours.

## The Real Efficiency Curve

Fourteen hours of OPC Loop produced a usable but rough UI. A mid-level frontend engineer would need two to three days for the same result. Sounds like AI wins?

Not quite. AI's fourteen hours included massive repetitive iteration—the same markdown rendering issue "fixed" three times, each time introducing new bugs differently. A human engineer would open the browser on first attempt, wouldn't make these mistakes. AI's advantage isn't higher quality, but working while you sleep.

The real efficiency formula isn't "AI is faster than humans," but "AI utilizes time when humans aren't available." Days I make design decisions, nights the loop implements, mornings I review. 24-hour utilization goes from 8 hours to 22 hours. That's OPC Loop's true value.
